# Friedrich Nietzsche - Period and Place Detail

## Daily Life Details

| Detail | Period | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Rose at dawn, typically between 5 and 6 AM; began the day with a cup of thick cocoa (oil-free) in enervating climates, or strong tea in small quantities | Wandering years, 1879-1889 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Strict daily schedule governing work, meals, and exercise; 5-7 hours of walking per day in Sils-Maria | Sils-Maria summers, 1881-1888 | Nietzsche-Haus Sils Maria archives |
| Carried small notebooks on walks; stopped to jot ideas while standing on paths, by lakes, on mountain trails | Wandering years, 1879-1889 | Stefan Zweig, biographical essay |
| Wrote for hours in his room, double glasses pressed close to the paper, fingers freezing, wrapped in overcoat and woolen scarf because the stove smoked but gave no warmth | Wandering years, 1879-1889 | Stefan Zweig, biographical essay |
| No coffee (believed it "spreads darkness"), no alcohol, no cigarettes, no cigars; drank only water from running brooks | 1879-1889 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Preferred places with running drinking water: Nice, Turin, Sils-Maria | 1879-1889 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| One glass of wine or beer was enough to "turn life into a valley of tears" | Lifelong | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| As a boy at Schulpforta, composed a long Latin essay in one night fueled by strong hot toddies, emulating Sallust's terseness | Schulpforta, 1858-1864 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Rented a simple room in the Durisch family farmhouse at Sils-Maria for one Swiss franc per day | Sils-Maria, 1881 and 1883-1888 | Paul Deussen, memoirs; Nietzsche-Haus archives |
| Personally selected and paid for dark green wallpaper in 1883 and a green tablecloth to soothe his eyes; devoted great attention to "the things that surround us" | Sils-Maria, 1883 onward | Nietzsche-Haus exhibition records |
| His room at Sils-Maria: a peasant-style table with coffee cup, eggshells, manuscripts, and toilet requisites in "a colorful jumble extending to the unmade bed, via a boot-jack with a boot jammed on it" | Sils-Maria, Sept. 1887 | Paul Deussen, memoirs of his Sept. 1887 visit |
| About five-foot-eight but a little stooped; almost blind; reserved, unaffected, and especially polite | 1880s | Stefan Zweig, biographical essay |
| Carefully studied the menu at each meal; checked whether tea was too strong, food too spiced; every dietary mistake caused days of digestive suffering | Wandering years, 1879-1889 | Stefan Zweig, biographical essay |
| In Nice, walked 6-8 hours per day on the presqu'ile of Saint-Jean, Mont Boron, and the path to Eze (now "Nietzsche's path") | Nice winters, 1883-1888 | Nietzsche's letters; Haussmann Real Estate historical account |
| Stayed at the Pension de Geneve on the Petite rue Saint-Etienne (now rue Rossini) in Nice for multiple winters | Nice, 1884-1888 | Nietzsche's letters; local historical records |
| Also lodged at 38 rue Segurane (2nd floor), Villa Mazzoleni (rue de Chateauneuf 39), 26 rue Francois de Paule, and 29 rue des Ponchettes | Nice, 1883-1888 | Nietzsche's letters |
| Left the Pension de Geneve after a fellow boarder humiliated him by offering to help cut his meat (his eyesight and coordination had deteriorated visibly) | Nice, c.1886-1887 | Provence 7 historical account |
| His room at the Pension de Geneve was on the fourth floor; it collapsed in the earthquake of 23 February 1887 (after he had written the third and fourth parts of Zarathustra there) | Nice, Feb. 1887 | Nietzsche Online historical records |
| In Turin, rented a room on the top floor at 6 Via Carlo Alberto from the Fino family; four-story building | Turin, 1888 | Lesley Chamberlain, Nietzsche in Turin |
| Turin routine: plain food, little alcohol (almost a teetotaler), rigorous daily exercise including gymnastics and long walks through Turin's arcades | Turin, 1888 | Lesley Chamberlain, Nietzsche in Turin; John Banville, NYRB |
| Turin memoranda to self: "Wait for tea to cool before drinking it, avoid all alcohol, crowds, reading, writing letters, wear warm clothes in the evening, eat rhubarb from time to time, have a napkin at breakfast, remember notebook" | Turin, 1888 | Lesley Chamberlain, Nietzsche in Turin |
| Avoided fashionable cafes, promenades, and bookshops for fear of bumping into acquaintances who might want to talk about Hegel | Turin, 1888 | Lesley Chamberlain, Nietzsche in Turin |
| Supported himself throughout the wandering years on a modest pension from the University of Basel (retired at 34) | 1879-1889 | Multiple biographies |
| Always had to find cheap quarters; budget was a constant constraint on his choice of lodgings | 1879-1889 | Letters; Stefan Zweig |
| Room furnishings during the wandering years: "no flower, no decoration. Back in a corner, a heavy and graceless wooden trunk, his only possession, with the two shirts and the other worn suit" | 1879-1889 | Stefan Zweig, biographical essay |
| On his tray: "innumerable bottles and jars and potions: against the migraines, against his stomach cramps, against spasmodic vomiting, against the slothful intestines, and above all the dreadful sedatives against his insomnia" | 1879-1889 | Stefan Zweig, biographical essay |
| Used chloral hydrate as his primary sedative for insomnia; also used Veronal | 1880s | Stefan Zweig; medical records |
| By 1882 was taking "huge doses of opium" and continued to have trouble sleeping | 1882 onward | Wikipedia, citing medical documentation |
| In 1883, writing his own prescriptions for chloral hydrate, signing them "Dr. Nietzsche" | Nice, 1883 | Wikipedia, citing Chamberlain |
| Also used potassium bromide (powerful sedative), hashish, and a mysterious "Javanese" preparation (probably opiate-based) | 1880s | Existenz journal, "The Case of Nietzsche's Madness" |
| Suffered from severe myopia since childhood; progressive retinal deterioration documented by specialists as extreme myopia with pigmentary retinal changes | Lifelong, worsening from 1870s | Journal of Neuropsychiatry; BIAPSY |
| During the Franco-Prussian War (1870), served as a medical orderly (ambulance corps); fell off his horse and was bedridden for about 9 months; contracted dysentery followed by chronic gastrointestinal disorders | 1870-1871 | Journal of Neuropsychiatry |
| Chronic migraines with aura phenomena, light hypersensitivity, vomiting, hypertonia of the digestive tract since schooldays at Schulpforta | Schulpforta onward, 1858- | Journal of Neuropsychiatry |
| Visited numerous physicians year after year; some prescribed medications with addictive potential, though he was considered never fully dependent | 1873 onward | Journal of Neuropsychiatry |
| Consulted Dr. Josef Wiel and attempted his diets for stomach patients; visited Wiel's health resorts; diagnosed with "nervous affection of the stomach connected with the head" | Basel period, 1875 | Nietzsche's letters (KSB 5); Wiel's Diatetisches Koch-Buch |
| "The best cooking is that of Piedmont" - preferred Piedmontese cuisine above all others | Turin, 1888 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Denounced German cooking: "Soup before the meal, meat cooked till the flavor is gone, vegetables cooked with fat and flour, the degeneration of pastries into paper-weights" | Lifelong complaint | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| English cooking also rejected: "basically repugnant to my instincts. It gives the intellect heavy feet, Englishwomen's feet" | General | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Principle: "A heavy meal is digested more easily than one that is too meager"; know the size of your stomach; no meals between meals | 1880s | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Warned against "interminable meals, which I call interrupted sacrificial feasts, and which are to be had at any table d'hote" | Wandering years | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| "Remain seated as little as possible; trust no thought that is not born in the open, to the accompaniment of free bodily motion" | General principle | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| "A sedentary life is the real sin against the Holy Ghost" | General principle | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| In 1882, purchased a Malling-Hansen Writing Ball typewriter (serial no. 125), the newest portable model with color ribbon, sent from Copenhagen | Genoa, Feb. 1882 | Klassik Stiftung Weimar; Malling-Hansen Society |
| Wrote a poem to the typewriter: "THE WRITING BALL IS A THING LIKE ME: MADE OF IRON / YET EASILY TWISTED ON JOURNEYS. / PATIENCE AND TACT ARE REQUIRED IN ABUNDANCE, / AS WELL AS FINE FINGERS, TO USE US." | 1882 | Nietzsche's papers; Kittler, Gramophone, Film, Typewriter |
| Typed the observation: "Our writing tools are also working on our thoughts" - anticipating McLuhan by 80 years | 1882 | Nietzsche's typewritten letter; Kittler |
| Abandoned the Writing Ball after about six weeks due to technical difficulties and portability issues; returned to handwriting and dictation | 1882 | Malling-Hansen Society research |
| Used Lou von Salome as a secretary/amanuensis; she took dictation, capturing his spoken rhythms in punctuation | 1882 | Kittler; various biographies |
| His style shifted from sustained arguments to aphorisms, puns, and "telegram style" - partly attributed to the typewriter's mechanical constraints | Post-1882 | Kittler, Gramophone, Film, Typewriter |
| Traveled constantly by railway across Switzerland, Italy, and southern France; the journey from Turin to Milan was short enough that he could detect changes in atmospheric humidity via his body | 1879-1889 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Considered his own body a "very delicate and reliable instrument" for reading climatic and meteorological influences | 1880s | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| In Nice, loved the Italian-speaking old town but had "a real aversion to the French part" which he found "insupportable" | Nice, 1883-1888 | Letters to Peter Gast |
| Read French literature voraciously: Maupassant, Bourget, the Goncourts, Taine, Baudelaire, Stendhal | Nice winters | Ecce Homo; letters |
| Attended the opera in Nice; immersed himself in the city's musical life | Nice, c.1886 | Haussmann Real Estate historical account |
| Preferred French culture above all: "I believe only in French culture, and regard everything else in Europe which calls itself 'culture' as pure misunderstanding" | General | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| Never read many books; a library made him ill; returned again and again to the same few old French authors | General principle | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| "To read a book early in the morning, at daybreak, in the vigor and dawn of one's strength - this is sheer viciousness!" | General principle | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| At Basel, his "whole intellectual routine, including my daily schedule, was an utterly senseless abuse of extraordinary powers" | Basel, 1869-1879 | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" |
| At Basel, taught classical philology; became the youngest person ever appointed to a full professorship there (age 24, without having completed a doctorate) | Basel, 1869 | Multiple biographies |
| Visited Tribschen (Wagner's villa near Lucerne) regularly during the Basel years; considered these "days of confidence, of cheerfulness, of sublime flashes, and of profound moments" | Basel, 1869-1872 | Ecce Homo |
| Spent a winter in Sorrento (1876-77) in a communal arrangement with Malwida von Meysenbug and Paul Ree at the Villa Rubinacci | Sorrento, 1876-1877 | Multiple biographies |
| His collapse: January 3, 1889, Turin, reportedly threw his arms around a horse being flogged in the Piazza Carlo Alberto, then collapsed in the street | Turin, Jan. 3, 1889 | Multiple contemporary accounts |
| After collapse: Franz Overbeck brought him to Friedmatt Sanatorium in Basel; transferred to mental clinic in Jena under Otto Binswanger; diagnosed with dementia paralytica | 1889 | BIAPSY; medical records |
| Final years in Weimar: cared for by his mother (until her death 1897) then by his sister Elisabeth; invalid, largely unresponsive | Weimar, 1897-1900 | Multiple biographies |
| Clothing: wore a heavy overcoat and woolen scarf indoors; owned two shirts and one other worn suit in his wooden trunk | Wandering years | Stefan Zweig |
| Dressed neatly but modestly; his stooped posture, thick mustache, and heavy spectacles were his defining physical features | 1880s | Contemporary descriptions |
| Had enormous, bushy mustache that became his visual trademark; it grew progressively larger through the 1880s | 1875-1889 | Photographs; contemporary accounts |
| Poor eyesight forced him to write in very large handwriting; his manuscripts became increasingly difficult to decipher | 1880s | Manuscript evidence |
| His mother Franziska sent him packages of food and clothing from Naumburg throughout the wandering years | 1879-1889 | Letters |
| Kept a spartan, almost monastic existence: no possessions beyond books, manuscripts, medicines, and the wooden trunk | Wandering years | Stefan Zweig |
| Insomnia was his most constant torment; the sedatives were "the only helpers in the empty silence of this strange room in which he never rests except in brief and artificially conquered sleep" | 1880s | Stefan Zweig |
| Gas lighting and oil lamps in his boarding-house rooms; electric street lighting was appearing in Turin by 1888 | 1880s | Period technology records |
| Communication by handwritten letters, constantly; maintained enormous correspondence with Overbeck, Gast, Rohde, his mother, his sister, Malwida von Meysenbug | Lifelong | Published correspondence (KSB) |
| Paid for his own book publications; editions of 40 copies at his own expense (Zarathustra Part IV, 1885) | 1885 | Publishing records |
| The table d'hote was the standard meal arrangement in boarding houses: fixed menu, fixed time, communal table with other boarders | Wandering years | Period practice; Ecce Homo reference |
| Took the waters at Recoaro (northern Italy) unsuccessfully before finding Sils-Maria | 1881 | Nietzsche-Haus archives |
| Experienced the Nice earthquake of February 23, 1887; his fourth-floor room in the Pension de Geneve collapsed | Nice, Feb. 1887 | Nietzsche Online |
| Bought himself a wood-burning stove (poele a bois) at 29 rue des Ponchettes in Nice; a notable personal expense | Nice, Jan. 1887 | Nietzsche's letters |
| Walked Turin's arcades (ten miles of covered walkways) daily; the city was "made for walking" | Turin, 1888 | John Banville, NYRB; Chamberlain |
| Practiced gymnastics in addition to walking; insisted on "equal importance of a philosophy of the body and of the mind" | Turin, 1888 | John Banville, NYRB |
| Could detect through bodily sensation the change in atmospheric humidity on the short train journey from Turin to Milan | 1880s | Ecce Homo |
| Composed the third part of Zarathustra during a strenuous uphill walk from the railway station to Eze, the Moorish village built among rocks above Nice | Nice, winter 1883-84 | Ecce Homo |
| During his final productive autumn (1888), wrote Twilight of the Idols, The Antichrist, Ecce Homo, and prepared The Wagner Case in rapid succession over about 70 days | Turin, autumn 1888 | Ecce Homo; publication records |
| "Never have my meals been more enjoyable, never has my sleep been better" - describing his final productive months before the collapse | Turin, autumn 1888 | Ecce Homo |
| Sent the "Wahnsinnsbriefe" (madness letters) - short, cryptic messages suffused with grandiosity - to Burckhardt, Overbeck, Cosima Wagner, and others in December 1888/January 1889 | Turin, Dec. 1888 - Jan. 1889 | BIAPSY; published letters |
| Leipzig cookery of his student days (1865): made him "gravely renounce my Will to Live"; described as the worst food of his life | Leipzig, 1865 | Ecce Homo |
| Opponent of vegetarianism "from experience - like Richard Wagner, who reconverted me" | General | Ecce Homo |
| Used the phrase "in vino veritas" ironically: "it seems that here too I disagree with the rest of the world about the concept 'Truth' - with me spirit moves on the face of the waters" | General | Ecce Homo |
| Childhood in Rocken: son of a Lutheran pastor; father died when Nietzsche was four; raised in a household of women (mother, sister, grandmother, two aunts) | Rocken/Naumburg, 1844-1858 | Multiple biographies |
| Schulpforta: elite Prussian boarding school with rigorous classical education; Latin, Greek, and strict discipline; the institution shaped his philological mastery | Schulpforta, 1858-1864 | Multiple biographies |
| The wine of Naumburg - his childhood hometown - was so bad it contributed to his lifelong suspicion of alcohol: "To believe that wine was exhilarating, I should have had to be a Christian" | Naumburg childhood | Ecce Homo |
| Venice: associated with music and tears; "I do not know how to make a distinction between tears and music. I do not know how to think of joy, or of the south, without a shudder of fear" | Venice visits | Ecce Homo |
| On his bridge poem and gondola songs: the sensory world of Venice (dark canals, golden lamp-drops, gondola music) directly entered his poetry | Venice | Ecce Homo |
| His friend Peter Gast (Heinrich Koselitz) served as his copyist, musical companion, and most loyal correspondent; Nietzsche called him "my Venetian maestro" | 1876-1889 | Ecce Homo; letters |
| Considered Paris the only possible home for an artist in Europe; valued "that subtlety of all the five senses which is the condition of Wagner's art" | General | Ecce Homo |
| Travel by second- or third-class railway compartments across European borders; customs, passport checks, currency changes at each frontier | 1879-1889 | Period travel conditions |
| Postal service was his lifeline: letters typically took 2-3 days within Europe; he sent and received multiple letters daily | 1879-1889 | Published correspondence |
| Hotel and pension pricing: his Sils-Maria room cost 1 Swiss franc/day; Nice pensions were similarly modest | 1880s | Deussen memoirs; Nietzsche's letters |
| Ate at the Hotel Alpenrose in Sils-Maria for lunch; the museum's furniture came from this hotel | Sils-Maria, 1881-1888 | Nietzsche-Haus exhibition |
| His seventy days of writing in autumn 1888: "no sign of tension, but on the contrary exuberant freshness and gayety" - a manic intensity that preceded the collapse | Turin, autumn 1888 | Ecce Homo |

## Period-Specific Language

| Expression | Meaning | Period | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bildungsphilister (cultural philistine) | Someone who mistakes cultural consumption for genuine cultivation; Nietzsche coined this as a term of contempt for the self-satisfied educated German bourgeoisie | Bismarck era, 1873 | Untimely Meditations I |
| Privatdozent | Unsalaried university lecturer who earned only from student fees; the entry-level academic rank in the German university system | 19th-century German academia | University system terminology |
| Ordentlicher Professor (Ordinarius) | Full professor with a salaried chair; what Nietzsche became at Basel at age 24, the youngest ever | German/Swiss academia, 1869 | University of Basel records |
| table d'hote | Fixed-menu communal meal at a set time in a boarding house or hotel; the standard arrangement for long-term pension guests | 19th-century European travel | Ecce Homo; pension culture |
| Pension / pensione | Boarding house with meals included; the standard accommodation for long-term visitors, intellectuals, and invalids on modest budgets across Switzerland, France, and Italy | 19th-century European travel | General period usage |
| chambre garnie | Furnished room for rent, typically without meals; a step below a pension | 19th-century France | Stefan Zweig |
| Kulturkampf | Bismarck's political struggle against Catholic institutions (1871-1878); literally "culture struggle" | Bismarck era, 1871-1878 | German political vocabulary |
| Reichsgrundung | The founding of the German Empire in 1871; the moment of national unification that Nietzsche saw as culturally catastrophic | 1871 | German political vocabulary |
| alla tedesca | "In the German style" - used in Italian cookbooks since the 16th century to describe soup served before the meal | Historical culinary term | Ecce Homo |
| Grunderzeit | The "founders' era" of rapid industrial expansion after 1871; the economic boom that followed unification | 1871-1873 | German cultural history |
| Grunderfieber / Grunderkrach | "Founders' fever" / "founders' crash" - the speculative bubble and crash of 1873 that ended the Grunderzeit euphoria | 1873 | German economic history |
| Wanderjahre | "Wandering years" - from Goethe's Wilhelm Meister; the term applied to Nietzsche's decade of nomadic existence between European boarding houses | Literary/biographical term | Biographical tradition |
| guter Europaer (good European) | Nietzsche's self-description and aspiration: a post-national intellectual who transcends parochial nationalism | Nietzsche's coinage, 1880s | Beyond Good and Evil |
| freier Geist (free spirit) | Nietzsche's term for the independent thinker liberated from tradition, morality, and national prejudice | Nietzsche's coinage, 1878 | Human, All Too Human |
| Uebermensch | "Overman" or "superman" - Nietzsche's concept of the human being who creates values beyond conventional morality | Nietzsche's coinage, 1883 | Thus Spoke Zarathustra |
| ewige Wiederkehr (eternal recurrence) | The idea that all events recur infinitely; came to him while walking at Sils-Maria beside the Silvaplana lake, August 1881 | Nietzsche's concept, 1881 | Ecce Homo; posthumous notes |
| Umwertung aller Werte (transvaluation of all values) | Nietzsche's projected grand work: the systematic overturning of Christian-moral value hierarchies | 1880s | Ecce Homo; notebooks |
| amor fati | "Love of fate" - Nietzsche's formula for greatness: wishing nothing altered, past or future | Nietzsche's concept, 1882 | The Gay Science; Ecce Homo |
| decadence | Used by Nietzsche in the French sense: cultural exhaustion, the decline of vital forces, the triumph of weakness over strength; applied to Wagner, Christianity, modern Europe | 1880s | The Wagner Case; Nietzsche contra Wagner |
| Zeitungs-Sklaverei (newspaper-slavery) | Nietzsche's term for the coercive influence of the press on public thought | 1880s | Posthumous notebooks |
| Kur / Kurort | "Cure" / "cure-place" - the spa or health resort culture that Nietzsche participated in (Recoaro, Baden-Baden, Steinabad) | 19th-century health culture | Letters |
| Nervenarzt | Nerve doctor; the 19th-century precursor to the neurologist/psychiatrist; Nietzsche consulted many | 19th-century medicine | Medical records |
| Dienstwohnung | Official residence provided with a university appointment; Nietzsche had one at Basel | German academic life, 1869 | University records |
| Kutscher / Fiaker | Coachman / cab driver; the standard urban transport before automobiles; horse-drawn cabs in all Nietzsche's cities | 19th century | General period usage |
| Vaterland | "Fatherland" - the nationalist term Nietzsche despised and mocked: "Peoples and Fatherlands" is the title of his satirical chapter in Beyond Good and Evil | Wilhelmine Germany | Beyond Good and Evil, ch. 8 |
| Stammtisch | "Regulars' table" at a tavern; the heart of German bourgeois social life, which Nietzsche avoided | 19th-century Germany | Cultural practice |
| Habilitation | Post-doctoral qualification required for professorship; the step Nietzsche skipped when Basel appointed him directly | German academic system | University records |
| Rektoratsrede | Rector's address; annual ceremonial speech at German universities; part of the ritual academic culture Nietzsche knew | German academia | University tradition |
| Philister | "Philistine" - Nietzsche's favorite insult for the complacent bourgeois; used in the university-slang sense from German student fraternities | Student culture, then Nietzsche's usage | Untimely Meditations |
| Couleur / Burschenschaft | Student fraternity colors/traditions; Nietzsche briefly joined the Franconia fraternity at Bonn before leaving in disgust | Bonn, 1864-1865 | Biographies |
| Kaiserreich | The German Empire (1871-1918); the political entity Nietzsche considered culturally destructive | 1871-1918 | Political vocabulary |
| Ressentiment | French word adopted by Nietzsche as a philosophical concept: the poisonous envy of the weak toward the strong | Nietzsche's technical usage, 1887 | Genealogy of Morals |
| Gai Saber / froehliche Wissenschaft | "Gay science" / "joyful wisdom" - Provencal troubadour term Nietzsche adopted for his philosophy of affirmation | Nietzsche's usage, 1882 | The Gay Science |
| Schwermuth | Melancholy, heaviness of spirit; 19th-century German psychological term Nietzsche used frequently in letters | 19th-century German | Letters |
| Reichsdeutsch | Citizen of the German Reich; Nietzsche renounced his Prussian citizenship to become a stateless person when he moved to Basel (1869) and never reacquired citizenship | Political/legal term, 1869 | Basel records |
| Wahnsinnsbriefe | "Madness letters" - the term scholars use for the short, grandiose messages Nietzsche sent in his final lucid/delirious days | Jan. 1889 | Biographical tradition |
| Oberengadin / Haute Engadine | The Upper Engadine valley where Sils-Maria is located; the Swiss mountain climate Nietzsche credited with saving his life | Geographic term | Ecce Homo; letters |
| Zarathustra | Not a neologism but Nietzsche's deliberate appropriation of the Zoroastrian prophet's name for his philosophical protagonist | 1883-1885 | Thus Spoke Zarathustra |
| Promenade des Anglais | The famous seafront walkway in Nice; built for English tourists and winter visitors; part of Nietzsche's walking world | Nice, 1880s | Geographic/cultural feature |
| Stiftsschule | Ecclesiastical boarding school like Schulpforta; rigorous classical education in the Prussian Gymnasium tradition | 19th-century German education | Educational history |
| Ordinariat | The formal appointment to a full professorial chair; what Nietzsche received without the usual Habilitation | German academia | University records |
| Dionysisch/apollinisch | Dionysian/Apollonian - Nietzsche's foundational cultural categories from The Birth of Tragedy, drawn from Greek religion but given new philosophical meaning | 1872 | The Birth of Tragedy |

## Commentary on His Times

| Commentary | Topic | Source | Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| "A great victory is a great danger. Human nature finds it harder to endure a victory than a defeat; indeed, it seems easier to achieve such a victory than to endure it in such a way that it does not turn into a signal defeat." | Franco-Prussian War victory | Untimely Meditations I | 1873 |
| "This error threatens to convert our victory into a signal defeat. A defeat? I should say rather, into the uprooting of the 'German Mind' for the benefit of the 'German Empire.'" | German unification destroying culture | Untimely Meditations I | 1873 |
| "Where is the new thought? Is it only a new combination of power? All the worse, if it does not know its own mind. Peace and laisser aller are not types of politics for which I have any respect." | The German Empire's intellectual emptiness | Peoples and Countries (Genealogy appendix) | 1887 |
| "I have seen proof, black on white, that Herr Dr. Forster has not yet severed his connection with the anti-Semitic movement... These accursed anti-Semite deformities shall not sully my ideal!" | Anti-semitism, his brother-in-law Forster | Draft letter to Elisabeth Nietzsche | Dec. 1887 |
| "Since then I've had difficulty coming up with any of the tenderness and protectiveness I've so long felt toward you. The separation between us is thereby decided in really the most absurd way." | Break with his sister over anti-semitism | Draft letter to Elisabeth Nietzsche | Dec. 1887 |
| "Have you grasped nothing of the reason why I am in the world? Now it has gone so far that I have to defend myself hand and foot against people who confuse me with these anti-Semitic." | Being confused with anti-semites | Draft letter to Elisabeth Nietzsche | Dec. 1887 |
| Proposes to "eject the anti-Semitic ranters from the country" | Political anti-semitism | Beyond Good and Evil, section 251 | 1886 |
| Suggests that the Jews "could have the supremacy over Europe" if they wanted it, but don't, and that their integration with European aristocracy would strengthen both | Jewish question | Beyond Good and Evil, section 251 | 1886 |
| "What I have never forgiven Wagner? The fact that he condescended to the Germans - that he became a German Imperialist. Wherever Germany spreads, she corrupts culture." | Wagner and German nationalism | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "Wagner is the counterpoison to everything essentially German - he is a poison, I do not deny it." | Wagner as anti-German paradox | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "They flatter our dangerous qualities. A stronger future is prepared for us in Goethe, Beethoven, and Bismarck than in these racial aberrations" (referring to Wagner and Schopenhauer) | Wagner and Schopenhauer as cultural dangers | Peoples and Countries | 1887 |
| "I am much more interested in a question on which the 'salvation of humanity' depends far more than upon any theologians' curio: the question of nutrition." | Theology vs. material life | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "All questions of politics, of the social order, of education, have been falsified from top to bottom, because the most harmful men have been taken for great men." | Political and social order | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "The press is a permanent false alert." | Journalism | Mixed Opinions and Maxims, sec. 321 | 1879 |
| "Profound contempt for those who work in the press" - listed as "Principle 1" | Journalists | Posthumous notebooks | 1884 |
| Reading newspapers is "a profound debasement." | Newspaper culture | Posthumous notebooks | 1880 |
| "Newspaper-slavery" - the coercive stranglehold of the press on public thought | Press and conformity | Posthumous notebooks | 1880s |
| The city of public opinion (the newspaper) "steams with the stench of slaughtered spirit" where souls are hung out to dry "like limp, dirty rags. And it is out of these rags that newspapers are made." | Press destroying spirit | Thus Spoke Zarathustra | 1883-85 |
| "God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him. How shall we comfort ourselves, the murderers of all murderers?" | Death of God as cultural event | The Gay Science, section 125 | 1882 |
| "The madman jumped into their midst and pierced them with his eyes. 'Whither is God?' he cried; 'I will tell you. We have killed him - you and I. All of us are his murderers.'" | Death of God; the Madman parable | The Gay Science, section 125 | 1882 |
| "What were we doing when we unchained this earth from its sun? Whither is it moving now? Away from all suns? Are we not plunging continually? And backward, sideward, forward, in all directions?" | Consequences of loss of God | The Gay Science, section 125 | 1882 |
| "This tremendous event is still on its way, still wandering; it has not yet reached the ears of men." | Death of God not yet understood | The Gay Science, section 125 | 1882 |
| "We who as children breathed the marshy atmosphere of the fifties, are necessarily pessimists with regard to the idea 'German'; we can be nothing else but revolutionaries." | Post-1848 German disillusionment | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "Suppose I were to step out of my house, and, instead of the quiet and aristocratic city of Turin, I were to find a German provincial town; my instinct would have to pull itself together to repel everything that would invade it from this downtrodden cowardly world." | German provincial life | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "Wherever Germany penetrates, she corrupts culture. It was the war which first 'redeemed' the spirit of France." | Franco-Prussian War and French culture | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "I believe only in French culture, and regard everything else in Europe which calls itself 'culture' as pure misunderstanding. It is hardly necessary to speak of the German variety." | European culture | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "With my nature, so alien to everything Teutonic that the mere presence of a German retards my digestion." | Anti-Teutonic sentiment | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "I shall never admit that a German can understand what music is. Those musicians, the greatest of them, who are called German, are all foreigners, Slavs, Croats, Italians, Dutchmen - or Jews." | German musical pretension | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "The climate of Germany, alone, is more than enough to discourage the strongest and most heroic intestines." | German climate and its effects | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "German intellect is indigestion; it can assimilate nothing." | German intellectual culture | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| "It was only the war which first redeemed the spirit of France" - meaning the defeat of 1870 forced France into genuine cultural renewal | French cultural recovery after 1870 | Ecce Homo | 1888 |
| "The problem of truthfulness is quite a new one. From this standpoint we regard such natures as Bismarck as culpable out of carelessness." | Bismarck and truthfulness | Peoples and Countries | 1887 |
| "Bismarck a Slav" - noting the Slavic elements in Prussian culture and Bismarck's personal character | Bismarck's character | Peoples and Countries | 1887 |
| "Anyone look upon the face of Germans. Everything that had manly, exuberant blood in it went abroad." | German emigration draining vitality | Peoples and Countries | 1887 |
| "I observe more inclination towards greatness in the feelings of the Russian Nihilists than in those of the English Utilitarians." | Russian nihilism vs. English utilitarianism | Peoples and Countries | 1887 |
| "England's small-mindedness is the great danger now on earth." | English culture | Peoples and Countries | 1887 |
| "The democratic movement is the heir to Christianity." | Democracy and Christianity | Beyond Good and Evil | 1886 |
| "Morality in Europe today is herd-animal morality." | European moral culture | Beyond Good and Evil, section 202 | 1886 |
| "Liberal institutions cease to be liberal as soon as they are attained: afterward there is nothing more thoroughly harmful to freedom than liberal institutions." | Liberalism | Twilight of the Idols, "Skirmishes" | 1889 |
| "The 'equality of souls before God,' this falsehood, this pretext for the rancune of all the base-minded, this explosive of a concept which eventually became revolution, modern idea, and the principle of the decline of the whole social order - is Christian dynamite." | Equality and Christianity | The Antichrist | 1888 |
| "Wagner sums up modernity. There is no help for it, one must first become a Wagnerian." | Wagner as symptom of modernity | The Wagner Case | 1888 |
| "Wagner's art is sick. The problems he presents on the stage - all of them problems of hysterics - the convulsive nature of his affects, his overexcited sensibility, his taste that required ever stronger spices." | Wagner and cultural sickness | The Wagner Case | 1888 |
| "The whole of the West no longer possesses the instincts out of which institutions grow, out of which a future grows." | Western institutional decline | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| "My conception of freedom. The value of a thing sometimes lies not in what one attains with it, but in what one pays for it - what it costs us." | Freedom's cost in modern society | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| "State is the name of the coldest of all cold monsters. Coldly it lies; and this lie creeps from its mouth: 'I, the state, am the people.'" | The modern state | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the New Idol" | 1883 |
| "Where the state ceases - there alone begins the human being who is not superfluous." | The state and human value | Thus Spoke Zarathustra | 1883 |
| "In large states public education will always be mediocre, for the same reason that in large kitchens the cooking is usually bad." | State education | Human, All Too Human | 1878 |
| "Socialism - as the logical conclusion of the tyranny of the least and the dumbest - is the final consequence of 'modern ideas' and their latent anarchism." | Socialism | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| "The scholar who actually does little else than welter in a sea of books finally loses completely the ability to think for himself." | Academic culture | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" | 1888 |
| On the university: "the scholar is a decadent" who responds only to stimuli from books and "no longer thinks himself" | University life | Ecce Homo | 1888 |
| "Convictions are more dangerous enemies of truth than lies." | Political conviction | Human, All Too Human | 1878 |
| "The surest way to corrupt a youth is to instruct him to hold in higher esteem those who think alike than those who think differently." | Conformism in education | The Dawn | 1881 |
| "Nationalism as it is understood today is a form of psychological regression." (paraphrase of scattered remarks on national identity as regression to tribal instinct) | Nationalism | Various works, 1880s | 1880s |
| "I am the last anti-political German." | His relation to German politics | Ecce Homo | 1888 |
| "Our writing tools are also working on our thoughts." - observation on technology's influence on consciousness | Technology and thought | Typewritten letter | 1882 |
| "I have now forty-three years behind me, and I am just as alone as I was when a child." | His isolation in the modern world | Letter to Malwida von Meysenbug | July 1888 |
| "The advent of nihilism" - he saw his own era, the 1880s, as the beginning of a civilizational crisis that would take centuries to unfold | Nihilism as historical diagnosis | Posthumous notebooks | 1887-1888 |
| "What I relate is the history of the next two centuries. I describe what is coming, what can no longer come differently: the advent of nihilism." | Predicting European nihilism | Posthumous notebooks (Will to Power preface) | 1887-1888 |
| "A good war hallows any cause." - spoken by Zarathustra, immediately undercut by context but widely (mis)quoted as endorsement | War and values | Thus Spoke Zarathustra | 1883 |
| "Against war it may be said that it makes the victor stupid and the vanquished revengeful." - his actual considered view, correcting the Zarathustra quip | War | Human, All Too Human | 1878 |
| "The worst readers are those who act like plundering soldiers: they take out a few things they can use, dirty and confuse the rest, and revile the whole." | Reading culture and misinterpretation | Mixed Opinions and Maxims | 1879 |
| "Every talent must unfold itself in fighting." | Competition in culture | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| "Without music, life would be a mistake." | Music in modern life | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| "If we have our own why of life, we shall get along with almost any how." | Modern conditions of existence | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| "Culture and the state are antagonists: the 'culture state' is merely a modern idea. One lives off the other, one thrives at the expense of the other." | Culture vs. state power | Twilight of the Idols | 1889 |
| On his era's anti-semites: "I am having all anti-Semites shot." (one of the Wahnsinnsbriefe, written in the delirium of early January 1889 - but expressing, in madness, the rage of his sane convictions) | Anti-semitism | Madness letter | Jan. 1889 |
